In this study, atomistic modeling leveraging ab initio calculations and a …
[Phys. Rev. Materials 10, 065601] Published Thu Jun 04, 2026

title: On improving experimental binding affinity predictions with synthetic data
abstract: The success of deep learning binding affinity prediction models depends critically on expanding experimental data with reliable synthetic data. We extend the Structurally Augmented IC50 Repository (SAIR) with physics-based computations and present two distinct data splits, SAIR-FEP and SAIR-OOD. With SAIR-FEP, we perform $\approx$80K absolute free energy perturbation calculations (AFEP) and curate two train/test splits to simulate realistic drug discovery scenarios. The free energy of binding and other physics-based computations are then used as either input features. We compare the performance of proteochemometric and state-of-the-art structure-based deep learning models and show that including physics-based features improves predictions, and that the quality of the structure plays a key role in their performance. For SAIR-OOD, we remove SAIR entries that overlap with complexes in public-facing benchmarks and demonstrate that simultaneous training on synthetic and experimental data improves performance on public-facing, experimental benchmarks.
relevance_reason: Uses absolute free energy perturbation (AFEP) calculations, an atomistic simulation method, to generate synthetic data for binding affinity prediction; satisfies (a) explicit atomistic systems, (b) simulation methodology (FEP), (c) application to improve predictions.

title: Effect of Ni/Cu ratio on the precipitation sequence and microstructural evolution in Fe–Cu–Ni–Al–Mn steel
abstract: This study systematically investigates the influence of Cu content on the co-precipitation behavior and sequence evolution of Cu-rich and NiAl phases in Fe–Cu–Ni–Al–Mn steels using a combined Monte Carlo and Molecular Dynamics simulation approach. The simulation results identify the Ni/Cu ratio is an important compositional variable affecting the relative precipitation tendency. Specifically, a critical threshold interval (Ni/Cu ≈ 2.4–2.6) is discovered: in steels with a low Ni/Cu ratio (2.6), the strong chemical ordering drives a NiAl-first sequence, forcing Cu to nucleate heterogeneously on pre-existing NiAl precipitates. These distinct precipitation pathways and morphological evolutions were successfully validated by Atom Probe Tomography experiments. Ultimately, this work clarifies how the Ni/Cu ratio regulates the synergy between precipitation strengthening phases, offering precise guidance for steel design.
relevance_reason: Combined Monte Carlo and Molecular Dynamics simulation on atomic configurations for precipitation, clearly satisfies (a)-(c); atomistic simulation application.

title: Computational investigation of agglomeration in PLA/glass nanocomposites
abstract: Agglomeration is a well-known phenomenon in polymer composites, and it has been studied with various computational methods including molecular dynamics (MD) simulation. However, to our knowledge, there have not been any studies modeling agglomeration in a polymer with the polymer consistent force field—INTERFACE force field (PCFF-IFF). IFF is an extension to PCFF designed for the accurate simulation of organic-inorganic interfacial interactions. In this study, we extended our previous work on MD simulation of PLA and glass nanocomposites to focus on nanoscale agglomeration of glass nanoparticles (NPs). We explored the effect of size (20 Å vs 31 Å diameter), number (1, 2, 5, 10, 15, 20, 100), and the packing (centered or random) of glass NPs on the mechanical properties of PLA/glass nanocomposites at 30 weight percent (wt%). Our MD predictions show that agglomerates at the nanoscale do not significantly impact the elastic moduli of the composite. Thus, it may be unnecessary for manufacturers to achieve a complete nanoscale dispersion of glass NPs in PLA, which may save both time and resources.
relevance_reason: Uses MD simulation with explicit atoms (PLA and glass NPs) and a force field; satisfies (a)-(c) as a classical MD application.

title: Accurate Tokenization of 3D Small Organic Molecules
abstract:
Atom-level generation of chemical structures, including drug-like molecules, is an increasingly active research direction. Due to the continuous nature of atomic coordinates, 3D structure generation has been mostly done with diffusion-style methods, with only a few attempts at leveraging autoregressive models. In this work, we develop CoordToken, a simple recipe to train tokenizers for 3D molecules.
We train CoordToken on two datasets: (i) on $\nabla^2$DFT where we obtain a $0.048$Å reconstruction error, which is a $4.2\times$ reduction compared to a prior method, and (ii) on a large corpus of 196M molecules, where we obtain micro averaged RMSD of $0.070$Å across all test datasets, including $0.074$Å on $\nabla^2$DFT. The tokenizer maintains near-perfect physical plausibility with a 98\% pass rate on the PoseBusters checklist. The tokenizer and the corresponding dataset are available at https://github.com/YerevaNN/CoordToken.
relevance_reason: Tokenizes 3D atomic coordinates with validation against DFT; satisfies (a)-(c) as a method tied to an atomistic potential.

title: Enhancing phase stability, thermodynamic, mechanical, and thermophysical properties of Ni3Ti intermetallic alloy through Nb substitution—a first principles study
abstract: The Ni-based intermetallic compounds play a vital role on enhancing properties of superalloy. One such intermetallic compound is Ni3Nb which exists in various crystal structures. In the present study, the stability of binary intermediate compound Ni3Nb in D0a and D022 structures is assessed via Density Functional Theory and their thermophysical properties are evaluated. Results show that even though the Ni3Nb-D0a phase is the most stable phase at 0 K but the metastable Ni3Nb-D022 exhibits superior physical properties. In addition, phonon calculations show that entropy and heat capacity of Ni3Nb-D0a are significantly greater than Ni3Nb-D0a structure irrespective of temperature. Another binary Ni-based intermetallic phase that has drawn significant interest is Ni3Ti owing to its stability and ability to retain its strength at high temperatures. However, it suffers from low fracture toughness and poor formability. The physical properties of the Ni3Ti binary phase can be further altered by substituting Nb at Ti sites in the Ni3Ti parent phase for all possible space groups of a chosen structure. However, there is a lack of structural information on the Ni3(Ti, Nb) ternary intermediate phase. The structural identification of the stable ternary intermediate compound Ni3(Ti0.67Nb0.33) is determined at 0 K by considering various possible structures, namely L12, D022, D019, D0a, D024, and R-phase. Among various chosen structures, the density functional theory calculations show that the Ni3(Ti0.67Nb0.33)-R phase is the most stable structure with the least energy of formation. Physical properties of Ni3(Ti0.67Nb0.33) are calculated using first principles calculations and compared with their respective binary phases, Ni3Nb-D0a and Ni3Ti-D024. The Pugh ratio of Ni3(Ti0.67Nb0.33)-R phase is ∼12% greater than the Ni3Ti-D024 which implies its greater ductility and formability than the binary phase. The hardness, Young’s modulus, thermal conductivity, and sound velocity of binary and ternary phases is of the order of Ni3Ti-D024 > Ni3(Ti0.67Nb0.33)-R > Ni3Nb-D022 > Ni3Nb-D0a. The structural stability of Ni3(Ti0.67Nb0.33) ternary R-phase at 300 K is further assessed via Ab initio Molecular Dynamics simulations.
relevance_reason: First-principles DFT study on crystal structures and phonons uses explicit atoms and DFT calculations, satisfying (a)-(c).

title: Taming the captodative glycyl radical for nickel-photocatalytic cross-coupling with alkyl chlorides
abstract:
Chem. Commun., 2026, 62,12678-12682
Rani Kumari, Ning Wei, Sebastian B. Beil
The captodatively stabilized glycyl radical was engaged in nickel photocatalysed C(sp3)–C(sp3) cross-coupling to obtain non-canonical amino acids. DFT-guided protecting group design enabled productive catalysis.
relevance_reason: DFT-guided protecting group design for cross-coupling; uses DFT (atomistic simulation) to guide experiments, meets (a)-(c).
